currentUse: Former church at 396 Church Street sold by the Archdiocese of Hartford after closure; interior gutted, nothing Lithuanian remains. Current occupant not verifiable from opened sources.
summary: The 1911 Romance Revival church of Šv. Andriejaus (St. Andrew Lithuanian) at 396 Church Street, New Britain, still stands but is no longer Catholic or Lithuanian in any respect. The parish appears under "Closed churches" in the Archdiocese of Hartford parish list with a 2017 closure — the year of the archdiocese-wide restructuring that cut 212 parishes to 127 — while directory records tie St. Andrew's phone and website to St. Francis of Assisi parish, whose current website lists only St. Francis, St. Jerome, and St. Maurice as worship sites, with no mention of St. Andrew. According to Global True Lithuania, the church was closed in the early 2020s, its interior was gutted, and the diocese sold the building together with the adjacent smaller former church building that had served as the John Rikteraitis Friendship Center for secular Lithuanian events; "nothing Lithuanian remains inside." The parish's most prominent recent association is with partisan commander Adolfas Ramanauskas-Vanagas, who was baptized there: a 3.5-meter monument planned for New Britain in 2017-2018 was never built, and a commemorative plaque unveiled inside the church in 2021 was removed soon after the church closed. A June 2026 Draugas article counts New Britain's Šv. Andriejaus among the Lithuanian parishes that "were closed or lost their Lithuanian face."

developments.headline: Parish listed as closed in the Archdiocese of Hartford's 2017 restructuring
developments.detail: The archdiocese reduced 212 parishes to 127 effective June 29, 2017. Wikipedia's list of Archdiocese of Hartford parishes places St. Andrew Church, New Britain ('for Lithuanian immigrants, became a parish in 1896') under 'Closed churches' with closure in 2017. Directory listings subsequently gave St. Andrew the same phone number and website as St. Francis of Assisi parish, indicating consolidation into that parish's structure.

sources.title: List of parishes in the Archdiocese of Hartford

developments.headline: Monument to Ramanauskas-Vanagas planned beside the New Britain Museum of American Art
developments.detail: Because Vanagas was born in New Britain (March 6, 1918) and baptized at St. Andrew's Church, the Lithuanian Genocide and Resistance Research Centre and the Nation's Fund planned a 3.5-meter oak-motif monument by sculptor Jonas Jagela for a park next to the New Britain Museum of American Art, with Lithuania's MFA covering delivery and installation. The monument was never built.

sources.title: Monument to Lithuanian guerrilla commander Vanagas planned in US

developments.headline: Vanagas plaque unveiled inside the church, then removed after closure
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania: 'In 2021, a commemorative plaque for him has been unveiled in the New Britain St. Andrew Lithuanian church but it was removed soon afterward after the church closed.'

developments.headline: Church closed, interior gutted, building sold by the diocese along with the Rikteraitis Friendship Center
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania reports the church 'was closed in the early 2020s, and the interior gutted,' and that the adjacent smaller former church building — the John Rikteraitis Friendship Center used for secular Lithuanian events — 'was by the diocese sold together with the church and nothing Lithuanian remains inside.' The buyer and sale price are not named in any source opened. The current St. Francis of Assisi parish website (stfranciscatholic.org) lists only St. Francis, St. Jerome, and St. Maurice as worship sites, confirming St. Andrew is no longer in Catholic use.

sources.title: St. Francis Catholic Parish - New Britain, CT

developments.headline: Draugas counts New Britain's Sv. Andriejaus among parishes closed or de-Lithuanianized
developments.detail: Vilija Jurgutiene's article 'Kam priklauso parapijos?' (Draugas, Vol. CXVII No. 47) lists New Britain Sv. Andriejaus alongside Philadelphia's Sv. Kazimiero and Sv. Jurgio as parishes that 'buvo uzdarytos arba prarado lietuviska veida' (were closed or lost their Lithuanian face).

sources.title: List of parishes in the Archdiocese of Hartford
Monument to Lithuanian guerrilla commander Vanagas planned in US
St. Francis Catholic Parish - New Britain, CT

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: One refinement, one unverified archive claim, no hard contradictions. (1) Closure date: the archive says closed ~end of 2021; Wikipedia's Archdiocese of Hartford parish list says St. Andrew 'closed in 2017.' These are reconcilable: the canonical parish was suppressed/merged in the June 2017 archdiocese-wide restructuring (consistent with the archive note that it 'survived review' as a worship site), while Global True Lithuania dates the physical church's closure to 'the early 2020s' and places the Vanagas plaque unveiling inside a functioning church in 2021 — supporting the archive's ~2021 date for the actual end of worship. The record should distinguish canonical closure (2017) from closure of the church building (~2021). (2) Buyer: the archive's 'sold to a Spanish-speaking Pentecostal church' could not be independently confirmed — Global True Lithuania confirms the diocese sold the church and adjacent Rikteraitis Friendship Center but does not name the buyer, and no property-record or news source naming the purchaser was findable. Unverified, not contradicted. (3) Plaque: removal after closure is confirmed by Global True Lithuania; the archive's added detail that it was 'not returned' is neither confirmed nor contradicted. Note also Wikipedia gives 1896 as founding/parish year (archive has founding 'not stated') and the current building dates to 1911 (Reiley & Steinback, per the Novelty Theater buildings directory).
gaps: Buyer identity, sale price, and sale year were not verifiable: searches of news, real-estate listings (Zillow/LoopNet), and Spanish-language congregation directories for 396 Church St found nothing naming the purchaser; New Britain land records (Vision Government Solutions assessor, cotthosting eSearch) are form-based databases not queryable by fetch, and the Archdiocese of Hartford's 2019 archives PDF that documented closure decrees now returns 404. The archive's 'Spanish-speaking Pentecostal church' buyer claim therefore remains unconfirmed — an Iglesia Universal de Jesucristo in New Britain was checked but its listed addresses (150 Production Ct, 683 Osgood Ave) do not match. No press coverage of the ~2021 closure itself was found in the New Britain Herald or Hartford Courant via web search. Current occupant and condition of the building as of 2026, and the fate/whereabouts of the removed Vanagas plaque, are unknown. Whether the 1896-founded parish's sacramental records went to St. Francis of Assisi parish could not be confirmed (the merged parish's site does not mention St. Andrew). No preservation efforts or community reunions surfaced in any search.
